model_narrative: We ship a product from several canning plants to several markets. For each plant and market we decide how much to ship, and because the per-unit transport cost falls as the shipped quantity grows, we also decide how that quantity is placed along a piecewise-linear cost curve by choosing which segment of the curve is active and how far into it the shipment reaches. The objective is to minimize the total transportation cost across all plant-market pairs.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the supply locations, each a canning plant that ships product out

sets.name: j
sets.doc: the demand locations, each a market that must receive product

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the ordered breakpoints of the piecewise-linear cost curve, listed from the lowest to the highest shipment level

sets.name: g
sets.doc: the segments of the piecewise-linear cost curve, one per interval between consecutive breakpoints; a subset of the breakpoints that excludes the final one

params.name: a
params.doc: the shipping capacity of each plant, in cases; the most that plant can send out in total

params.name: b
params.doc: the demand at each market, in cases; the least that market must receive in total

params.name: c
params.doc: the transport cost coefficient for each plant-market pair, in thousands of dollars, applied per unit of the square-root shipment measure

params.name: p
params.doc: the shipment quantity at each breakpoint of the cost curve, in cases; the x-coordinate of that breakpoint

params.name: nseg
params.doc: the width of each segment along the shipment axis, in cases; the gap in shipment quantity between a segment's breakpoint and the next breakpoint

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the shipment quantity sent from a plant to a market, in cases

vars.name: sqrtx
vars.doc: the square-root measure of the shipment quantity for a plant-market pair, used to price the concave economy-of-scale cost

vars.name: seg
vars.doc: the amount of shipment placed within a given segment of the cost curve for a plant-market pair, as a fraction-like fill of that segment

vars.name: gs
vars.doc: the segment-selection indicator for a plant-market pair; equals 1 if that segment is the active one and 0 otherwise

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the total transportation cost over all plant-market pairs, in thousands of dollars

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Each plant cannot ship out more than it is able to. For every plant, the total shipment sent from it across all markets must not exceed that plant's shipping capacity.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Every market must have its demand met. For each market, the total shipment arriving into it from all plants must be at least that market's required amount.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: For each plant and market, the shipment quantity must equal the level reconstructed from the cost-curve segments. Add up, over all segments, the shipment level at each segment's starting breakpoint counted when that segment is the active one, plus the segment width scaled by how far the shipment reaches into that segment, and set the shipment quantity equal to that total.

constraints.name: seg[i,
constraints.intent: A segment can only be filled if it is the active segment. For each plant, market, and segment, the amount of shipment placed within that segment must not exceed the indicator that marks that segment as active.

constraints.name: gs[i,
constraints.intent: For each plant and market, at most one segment of the cost curve may be chosen as active. Across all segments, the selection indicators add up to no more than one.

constraints.name: z
constraints.intent: The total transportation cost adds up the priced shipment over every plant-market pair. For each pair, multiply that pair's cost coefficient by its square-root shipment measure, sum these across all plant-market pairs, and set the total cost equal to that sum.

query: We're weighing a plan to lean harder on the Seattle plant — push its combined outbound volume across all three markets up to at least 350 cases. Plug that in and tell me where the total shipping bill lands.
expected_pyomo: model.sea_total_floor = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x['seattle', j] for j in model.j) >= 350)

query: There's a proposal to keep San Diego from running both of its workhorse lanes — the New York haul and the Topeka haul — in the same top size bracket at once; at most one of those two lanes should sit in that largest segment. Run it and walk me through the new total cost.

query: Engineering is pitching a cap on the San Diego-to-Topeka lane so it never sits in any of the three largest size brackets — none of the top segment selectors on that lane should be switched on. Plug that in and tell me what it does to the total cost.

query: There's a proposal on the table to spread Seattle's output more evenly — no single Seattle lane should carry more than 200 cases, applied to every market it serves. Run that and tell me the resulting total cost.

query: We're looking at a two-part scenario: route at least 100 cases of New York's demand through Seattle, and at the same time hold the San Diego-to-Topeka lane down to no more than 200 cases. Add both together and tell me what the total shipping cost becomes.

query: Topeka leans entirely on San Diego while Seattle never touches that market. Shouldn't Seattle at least be carrying as much of Topeka's load as San Diego does, rather than none of it? What's the model seeing that keeps Seattle off that lane?

query: The whole Chicago order rides on Seattle while San Diego stays out of that market entirely. Wouldn't it make sense for both plants to share Chicago — each one moving at least 100 cases into it? What's the trade-off that's pushing Chicago all onto a single plant?

query: San Diego is hauling more than 300 cases into some markets all on its own. Shouldn't no single San Diego lane be allowed to run above 250 cases, across every market it serves? What's keeping the optimizer from spreading San Diego's load out that way?
